coredata.dc:title: Surface state selective tunable emission of graphene quantum dots exhibiting novel thermal quenching characteristics
coredata.dc:creator: Dey, Tamal
Mukherjee, Subhrajit
Ghorai, Arup
Das, Soumen
Ray, Samit K.
coredata.dc:description: Highly luminescent, crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of homogenous size and shape with high yield have been successfully synthesized by a one-pot, facile and rapid synthesis technique. The characteristic blue emissions of GQDs from the crystalline sp2 graphene core could be tuned from green to yellow wavelength, by modulating sp3-oxygeneous surface states. The progressive evolution of surface functional groups on annealing at different temperatures has been corroborated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Temperature dependent as well as excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of the samples exhibits distinctly different thermal quenching characteristics. A transition from positive to negative thermal quenching phenomena with progressive annealing up to 300 οC and crossovers at intermediate temperatures have been observed. With the help of schematic energy band diagram, we have explained the novel fluorescence mechanism in GQDs. Au/GQD/Au UV-photodetector device has been fabricated and its performance has been studied. Tunable emission of solution processed GQDs from UV to red wavelength can be very attractive for their use in bio-imaging and optoelectronics devices.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and compositional properties of GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Tamal Dey acknowledges DST, Govt. of India for providing INSPIRE Fellowship ( IF-160592 ). Authors acknowledge the partial support from DST-CERI “ICF” project grant. The use of XPS facility under the DST FIST project at Department of Physics, IIT Kharagpur is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing evolution of the chemical structure during pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) in GQD formation. (a) Molecular structure of CA, (b) intermediate products formed during pyrolysis of CA, (c) GQDs formed by incomplete carbonization. Digital images showing the physical change of citric acid with time while heating at 200 °C. (d) Citric acid as-purchased from Sigma Aldrich, (e) after 10 min in molten condition, (f) after 30 min, the orange solution indicates formation of GQDs, (g) prolonged heating results in the formation of a black, dense solution of graphene oxide. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Structural characteristics of GQDs. (a) TEM image of as-synthesized GQDs. Inset shows the size distribution of GQDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image showing lattice fringes. (c) SAED pattern reveals polycrystalline nature of the sample. (d) AFM image with height profile providing the estimation of thickness. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XRD pattern of as-synthesized (RT) sample. Binding energy of C 1s electrons of (b) as-synthesized and (c) 300 °C annealed GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical absorption and phonon spectra of GQDs. (a) UV–visible absorption spectrum. (b) Raman spectra of samples annealed at different temperatures. (c) Stokes and anti-Stokes (30 times magnified) spectra for the estimation of phonon temperature. (d) Variation of phonon temperature with the power of laser source. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical emission characteristics of different GQDs samples. (a) Room temperature PL of the as-prepared (RT) sample revealing two Gaussian peaks upon deconvolution corresponding to HOMO-LUMO core state emission (peak-1) and the emission from the surface states (peak-2). (b) Normalized room temperature PL of all the samples annealed at different temperatures showing red-shift of the peak emission wavelength with increasing anneal temperature. (c) and (d) Excitation dependent PL emission of the as-prepared sample and the sample annealed at 300 °C, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Relation between PL peak position and different function groups. Energy band diagram for (b) as-synthesized GQDs and (c) sample annealed at 300 °C. It is noted that higher surface state and trap state density are present in the annealed sample.

Photoluminescence quenching characteristics. (a) Positive thermal quenching (PTQ) characteristics of the as-synthesized (RT) sample, (b) Negative thermal quenching (NTQ) characteristics of the sample annealed at 300 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photodetection properties of the Au/GQD/Au device. (a) Schematic device structure (not to scale). (b) Current-voltage characteristics in both dark and illuminated conditions. (c) Photoresponse behavior of the device under switching optical illumination. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Evolution of oxygen functional groups with anneal temperature.
Variation of PL peak position with sp2/sp3 ratio.

head.ce:title: Surface state selective tunable emission of graphene quantum dots exhibiting novel thermal quenching characteristics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Technology Development Centre, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Currently at S. N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences, Salt lake, Kolkata, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly luminescent, crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of homogenous size and shape with high yield have been successfully synthesized by a one-pot, facile and rapid synthesis technique. The characteristic blue emissions of GQDs from the crystalline sp2 graphene core could be tuned from green to yellow wavelength, by modulating sp3-oxygeneous surface states. The progressive evolution of surface functional groups on annealing at different temperatures has been corroborated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Temperature dependent as well as excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of the samples exhibits distinctly different thermal quenching characteristics. A transition from positive to negative thermal quenching phenomena with progressive annealing up to 300 οC and crossovers at intermediate temperatures have been observed. With the help of schematic energy band diagram, we have explained the novel fluorescence mechanism in GQDs. Au/GQD/Au UV-photodetector device has been fabricated and its performance has been studied. Tunable emission of solution processed GQDs from UV to red wavelength can be very attractive for their use in bio-imaging and optoelectronics devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a single atomic layer of graphite, has drawn massive attention due to some of its unique features, such as, superior intrinsic mobility (∼105 cm2/V.s) [1], and excellent electrical [2] and thermal conductivities [3]. However, the lack of an energy gap in band structure and high transparency [4] precludes its utilization for applications in optoelectronic devices. Several efforts have been made globally to introduce a finite energy gap in graphene, either by surface functionalization [5,6] or by utilizing spatially confined carbon nanostructures, including graphene nanoribbons [7], carbon nanotubes (CNT) [8], carbon dots [9] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [10]. Among all these carbon derivatives, the newest member of the family, zero dimensional spatially confined graphene quantum dots have drawn considerable attention owing to their various alluring properties, such as, high surface to volume ratio, size dependent optical properties, bright excitonic fluorescence, strong excitonic absorption bands extending to the visible region, and long term photo-stability [11–13]. This emerging material has a prospect of utilization in wide range of potential applications, like, optoelectronic and photovoltaic devices [14,15], super capacitors [16], quantum computing [17], catalysis and sensors [18]. These GQDs can also be a perfect candidate in the field of biological labeling or bio-imaging for medical diagnosis, bio-sensors and drug delivery due to low cytotoxicity, ultrahigh emission, low photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility, as compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots [19–22].

Various approaches to synthesis GQDs can be primarily classified into top-down and bottom-up processes. Synthesized GQDs produced by top down methods usually exhibit highly defective structures and rather low quantum yields with unavoidable difficulty in size control in nanometer scale [23], limiting their application in bio-photonics and optoelectronic devices. Several attempts have been made through bottom-up approaches using the carbonization of various organic precursors [24,25] such as aromatic molecules or benzene derivatives offering advantages of precise size control, morphology and relatively better yield. The emission from GQDs can be modified from blue to green to red color by controlling their size, shape, edge states, surface states, doping, and attached functional groups [11,26–29]. Intrinsic GQDs exhibiting blue luminescence upon UV excitation are harmful for living cells or bio-systems. Hence GQDs with tunable visible (green, yellow or red) emission, as reported in inorganic semiconductor nanocrystals [30], are critically required for bio-labelling applications.

Here, we adopted a facile and rapid method to synthesize water soluble, crystalline, homogenous GQDs exhibiting excellent optical properties with high yield and excellent stability [24]. Synthesized GQDs exhibit tunable emission from blue to yellow wavelength by inducing the defect states within the band gap. The subsequent tailoring of surface oxygen functional groups by thermal annealing at different temperatures, which is a very simple and cost-effective method, results in gradual red-shift of the emission and long term photo-stability. The origin of tunable optical emission and the detailed photo-physical mechanisms are presented. Synthesized GQDs also exhibit novel thermal quenching characteristics showing both positive and negative behavior in same material, controllable by varying the density of surface states and core state emission centers. The results obtained may pave the way towards the development of new techniques based on engineering surface defect states to modulate various properties of nanostructured materials in a wide variety of applications.
Citric acid (C6H8O7) (Sigma-Aldrich) and sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH) (Sigma-Aldrich) were used for the synthesis of GQD. Acetone and propanol was used for cleaning purpose.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully prepared by a simple, cost effective, one step chemical process reported elsewhere [24], where the thermochemical decomposition (pyrolysis) of citric acid (CA) was done at a relatively low temperature. A synthetic scheme is presented in Fig. 1 (a–c), highlighting the chemical steps. Initially 2 g CA was taken in an alumina crucible and heated at 200 οC in ambient atmosphere for different function time, as shown in Fig. 1(d–g). After 5 min, the citric acid was liquefied and the solution color subsequently changed from colorless as shown in Fig. 1(e) to deep orange in about 40 min as presented in Fig. 1(f), indicating the formation of GQDs. Prolonged heating was avoided to prevent the formation of black colored graphene oxide as shown in Fig. 1(g).

The CA, as a precursor, has very important role to synthesize highly luminescent GQDs over other precursor as described in literature [31]. Six-membered carbon ring structure formation is essential for high luminescence properties and CA results in six-membered ring structure by the aggregation of CA monomers during pyrolysis. At the melting temperature citric acid (CA) decomposes and the hydronium ions are formed from the CA which acts as a catalyst for further decomposition of CA. In the second step, at high temperature, polymerization and condensation takes place giving rise to soluble polymers. Thereafter, aromatization occurs through cycloaddition and aldol condensation reaction followed by nucleation. When the concentration of aromatic cluster reaches a super saturation point GQDs are formed [32].

As-prepared orange colored product was dissolved in 20 mL in water and 1 (M) sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution was added drop wise under vigorous stirring till the pH value reached to 7.0 (neutral solution). NaOH is also beneficial in dehydrolysis of citric acid and controlling the size of the water soluble GQDs. The estimated synthesized yield was ∼12.25% higher than reported values in literature [33,34]. Synthesized GQDs were dispersed in water and centrifuged several times to wash out the unwanted Na+ ions to obtain impurity free samples. As-prepared GQD solution was stored for several months without showing any degradation (please see Fig. S1 in ESI copy).

The morphology of as-synthesized GQDs was investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) [JEOL, JEM-2100F] and atomic force microscopy (AFM), [Agilent Technology, 5500 AFM]. X-ray diffraction patterns of the samples were recorded using a Phillips MRD X-Ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 10º-30°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (model PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, INC, Japan), with an incident X-ray of energy 1.48 KeV (Al-Kα) was utilized to study the chemical composition and functional groups attached to GQDs. Elemental composition mapping was done by Auger electron spectroscopy (AES) (PHI 710). For HRTEM, small amount of sample was drop-casted on commercially available carbon coated copper grid and dried naturally. For XPS, AES, XRD and AFM studies, the synthesized sample was drop casted on silicon substrate and dried naturally. After that they were annealed at required temperatures (100, 200 and 300 °C). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy of GQDs were carried out in solution phase using a fiber probe based Avaspec-3648 spectrometer combined with broadband Deuterium and Halogen excitation sources. For PL studies, GQD solutions were drop casted on four different substrates and all of them were dried naturally. The control sample was kept separate (labeled as RT) and the others were annealed at different temperatures (100 °C, 200 °C and 300 °C) for 2 h each and labeled accordingly. Temperature dependent steady state photoluminescence of the samples were studied using a JANIS (model CCS-510) closed cycle cryostat, a He-Cd Laser of excitation wavelength of 325 nm and a monochromator [TRIAX-320] equipped with a photomultiplier detector [Hamamatsu R928]. Raman spectroscopy was carried out with an Argon laser (excitation wavelength 514 nm) and a spectrometer (Jobin Yvon Horiba T64000).

For the device fabrication, 1 × 1 cm2 SiO2 substrates were used and cleaned by ultra-sonication in DI water, acetone and propanol for 15 min each to remove oil and other organic impurities. As-synthesized GQDs were spin-coated on top of a freshly prepared SiO2 substrate at 3000 rpm for 4–5 times to ensure good coverage of the quantum dots over a large area. Finally, gold (Au) metal electrodes were thermally deposited on top of the device at a base pressure of 3 × 10−6 mbar to complete the device fabrication. The effective area of each photodetector devices was 0.8 mm2. The current-voltage (I−V) characteristic of the fabricated heterojunction device has been studied using a probe station system equipped with programmable DC voltage generator (ADCMT 6144) and a Pico-ammeter (Keithley 6485). He-Cd Laser with 325 nm radiation has been used as light source for photoresponse studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and compositional properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Typical TEM micrograph presented in Fig. 2 (a) exhibits almost spherical shaped monodispersed GQDs over a large area. The lateral size distribution of the synthesized GQDs estimated using the ‘ImageJ’ software is displayed in the inset of Fig. 2(a), showing an average size of GQDs is ∼4 nm. Fig. 2(b) shows the HR-TEM micrograph of as-prepared GQDs, where the lattice fringes indicate the formation of crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs). A set of lattice planes with 0.24 nm spacing is observed corresponding to the (1120) planes in the hcp unit cell of GQDs [31]. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, as displayed in Fig. 2(c), exhibits the diffraction spots similar to the graphene sheets. Results reveal that graphene samples retain their local crystalline symmetry also in quantum dots similar to 2D graphene layers.

The AFM micrograph in Fig. 2(d), with corresponding histogram in the inset, reveals the average thickness of the GQDs to be ∼4 nm. X-ray diffraction spectrum of GQDs deposited on Si is shown in Fig. 3 (a). A characteristic peak at 27° related to (002) plane (JCPDS card no. 75–1621) indicates the formation of graphene nanostructures by pyrolysis of citric acid, although a much broader peak suggests disordered structures of GQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been carried out in order to qualitative and quantitative investigation of the constituents, chemical bonding and the associate functional groups of as-synthesized and annealed GQD samples. Fig. 3(b) and (c) represent high resolution core-level XPS spectra of C 1s electrons for as-prepared and 300 °C annealed GQD samples, respectively. The intense and broad C 1s peak could be deconvoluted using Gaussian fitting, into three distinct peaks centered at 284.6 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.8 eV, which are attributed to core carbon groups (C=C) corresponding to sp2 chemical bonding states, carbonyl (C=O)/hydroxyl (C–OH)/epoxy (C–O–C) groups and carboxyl groups (-COOH) corresponding to sp3 bonding, respectively. These are in well agreement with the previously reported results [35]. It is further confirmed from C 1s spectra that the density of surface functional groups increases with increasing anneal temperature. The nature of bonding can be evaluated by taking the ratio of the integrated peak areas corresponding to the C=C and other peaks to the total area under the C 1s spectrum, using Shirley background correction [36]. The percentage of three different carbons groups estimated from XPS spectra have been presented in Table 1
for all the samples. The relative fraction of the sp2 bonded carbon content has been found to be 78%, 69%, 53% and 37%, for as-synthesized and annealed samples at 100 οC, 200 οC and 300 οC, respectively. A significantly increased fraction of sp3 carbon bonds from oxygeneous functional groups is observed, which increases on increasing the anneal temperature (see Table 2).

The elemental auger electron spectroscopy (AES) analysis of as-synthesized and annealed GQDs, as shown in Fig. S2 (please see the ESI copy), has been used to extract the C and O content quantitatively, which is found to be consistent with the XPS data. Apparently, the auger mapping in Fig. S2(a) and Fig. S2(b) shows that the relative amount of the oxygen content increases significantly with the enhancement of annealing temperature. The results are present in Table 1 showing reduced carbon content on increasing the anneal temperature. Combined results of the XPS and AES reveal that surface defects are introduced into GQDs upon annealing the samples at higher temperature.

Typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized GQDs in aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 4 (a). Two well resolved pronounced excitonic absorption bands centered at ∼277 nm and ∼345 nm with a long absorption tail extending to 600 nm could be observed. A strong characteristic absorption peak centered at ∼277 nm is attributed to the π-π* electrons transition in the molecular orbital of C=C of the sp2 hybridized carbon domains from the core cluster of GQDs [37]. A nominal shoulder in absorption spectrum, in the vicinity of the strong absorption peak has been observed at ∼345 nm, which possibly arises due to the n-π* electrons transition in the molecular orbital level of functional groups, related to the sp3 clusters and can be considered as the defect state absorption [38]. The defect states appearing between the π–π* band are attributed mainly to the oxygen containing functional groups. Comparatively broader nature of the shoulder peak at ∼345 nm could originate due to the closely packed energy levels of different functional groups, such as, C=O, -COOH, and C-OH etc. This illustrates that synthesized GQDs consist of a few layers of graphene sheets at the core, which exhibits quantum size effect and a shell formed by the sp3 clusters of oxygenous functional groups. Fig. 4(b) displays the Raman spectra of as-synthesized and annealed GQD samples acquired upon 514 nm laser excitations. Two distinct Raman features observed at ∼1342 cm−1 and ∼1590 cm−1 for as-synthesized sample, represent ‘D’ and ‘G’ band, respectively with an intensity ratio ID/IG of 0.99. The disordered ‘D’ band arises due to the breathing mode of clustering hexagonal aromatic rings, which are not active in the single crystalline graphite symmetric sites [39]. But the existence of defects in GQDs allows this mode to be Raman active, whereas, the ‘crystalline’ G-band is attributed to the primary in-plane vibrational mode caused by LO E2g phonons at Γ-point [40].

For the sample annealed at 300 °C the D-peak is shifted to ∼1338 cm−1. This decrease of phonon frequency may be due to the softening of lattice caused by increased defect fraction due to annealing [41,42]. The broadening of the D-band compared to the G-band is one of the main evidences of the presence of aromatic sp3-carbon disorder states. Therefore, the band width characterizes the degree of disorder in carbon structures due to the increased fraction of edge carbon atoms bonded to the functional groups. During the ambient oxidation at higher temperatures more oxygen-containing functional groups are introduced at the edges and basal planes of the constituent graphene sheets, resulting in higher disordered structure. The comparative intensities of D band and G band (ID/IG ∼1) for as-produced GQDs are found to be similar to that of high-quality reduced few layer graphene sheets [39]. This indicates that the functionalization of GQDs does not significantly affect their quality.

During PL and other measurements, the incident laser beam can locally heat the sample. Thus, optical properties can be affected, which is undesirable in our present study. Hence, clarification is necessary whether the laser irradiation produces heat and alters spectroscopy data. The Stokes and anti-Stokes intensity ratios are very useful to monitor unwanted local heating effect and the local phonon temperature during laser irradiation in Raman spectroscopy [43,44]. The effect of local heating in the sample while irradiating with high power laser beam has been investigated and analyzed. For this purpose we have measured both Stokes and anti-Stokes shift in Raman spectroscopy with varying power of the laser beam on as synthesized GQDs sample. In Fig. 4(c), Stokes and anti-Stokes Raman shift are shown for a laser power of 250 mW. The intensity ratio of the Stokes (Is) and anti-Stokes (IAS) signal at a certain frequency provides the phonon temperature using the following equation [45],
(1)
I
A S
I
S
=
(
ω
A S
ω
S
)
4
exp
( −
h ν
k
B
T
)

Here ν is the average Raman shift of the peaks, ωAS and ωS are the frequency of the corresponding anti-Stokes and Stokes lines, respectively. We have used the data corresponding to the ‘G’-peak only because the ‘D’-peak intensity is very small in anti-stokes regime. Phonon temperature has been calculated using equation (1) and the variation of the phonon temperature with different incident laser powers has been shown in Fig. 4(d). Saturation of the local temperature at a higher power presumably reflects the rate of perpendicular heat flow to the underlying substrate [46]. However, use of non-conducting substrate for optical measurements leads to the accumulation of generated heat by laser irradiation which effectively increases the local temperature inside the samples resulting in changes of optical properties of GQDs [44].

To further explore the optical properties of the water soluble GQDs, the steady state photoluminescence (PL) measurements have been carried out systematically. The most distinctive feature of GQDs apart from other carbon materials is that they emit a specific color, which is tunable and non-blinking over a long time. Fig. 5 (a) displays the room temperature PL spectrum for as-synthesized GQDs which clearly exhibits two well resolved Gaussian peaks upon deconvolution. The intense one is centered at ∼450 nm (peak-1) and the relatively weaker one, is at ∼500 nm (peak-2), for as-synthesized GQDs. The shift of peak positions with change in anneal temperature is depicted in Fig. 5(b). The position of the peak-1 changes negligibly, whereas, peak-2 is gradually red-shifted from ∼500 nm to ∼545 nm, with increasing annealing temperature from room temperature to 300 °C. The intensity of the peak-2 increases as compared to that of the peak-1, with annealing temperatures.

Fig. 5(c) and (d) show the PL behavior of the RT sample and the sample annealed at 300 οC, respectively, under excitations of different wavelengths. Notably with increasing excitation wavelength, the intensity of peak-1 gradually diminishes with respect to peak-2, as shown in Fig. 5(c). It is due to the fact that a higher wavelength excitation is not enough to excite electrons from the HOMO state to LUMO state, whereas, the relative intensity increment of peak-2 implies the energy required to transition of electrons inside the surface defect states is lower than the HOMO to LUMO transitions. The results demonstrate the tunability of PL emission in GQDs using a simple annealing process, without the requirement of any functionalization or chemical doping.

Since the dominant emission is from radiative shallow trap states for the annealed samples, the emission peak doesn't shift significantly with the excitation wavelength (Fig. 5(d)) because lots of shallow traps are in close proximity. Thus the emission is independent of the excitation wavelength in annealed samples, which is of practical importance in bio-imaging and labeling. Different excitation sources are used in different labs and diagnosis clinics; hence it would be a problem if the photoluminescence emission is dependent on the excitation wavelength. Also, the visible lights can also be used as an excitation source instead of UV light sources which are harmful for living cells. It may be noted that achieving an excitation independent emission from GQDs using a simple and cost effective thermal annealing process hasn't been reported so far.

The peak-1 originates from electronic transition from the quantum mechanically confined carbon core states and the other one (peak-2) is ascribed to the emission from radiative shallow traps. As observed from XPS analysis, at a higher annealing temperature more and more oxygen functional groups (surface states) are introduced, which results in a red-shift of peak-2. The thermal expansion coefficient of C-C and C=C bonds are very small (∼10−6 K−1) leading to negligible change in the dimension of core molecules at a higher anneal temperature. Hence the red shift of the emission with anneal temperature can be correlated to increased surface functional groups as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The schematic energy band diagrams for two extreme samples (RT and 300 °C annealed) are respectively presented in Fig. 6(b) and (c) respectively to gain more information about the fluorescence mechanism in GQDs. The possible mechanism of the tunable emission could be attributed to the transition from higher energy absorbing states to lower energy emissive states.

To have more insights on the emission mechanism, we performed temperature dependent PL study with the measurement temperature varying from 10 K to 300 K for the as-prepared sample (RT) and samples annealed at different temperatures (100 °C, 200 °C and 300 °C). The temperature dependent PL spectra of two extreme samples (RT and 300 °C) are shown in Fig. 7 (a) and Fig. 7(b), respectively. For the as-synthesized (RT) sample, as the measurement temperature increases, the intensity of the emission reduces gradually. This is known as positive thermal quenching (PTQ), which is known to be caused by thermally activated non-radiative trapping of excited carriers. At a higher temperature, large numbers of excited carriers relax through non radiative effects, e.g., defect-related deep trap states.

The decrease of non-radiative recombination lifetime with increase in temperature reduces the quantum efficiency of radiative recombination and PL emission intensity. Quite interestingly, the behavior of annealed samples is rather different, which exhibit anomalous PL quenching behavior. For the sample annealed at 300 °C, the intensity of PL emission increases with measurement temperature. This behavior is known as ‘negative thermal quenching’ (NTQ) effect. For the GQDs, PL emission exhibits a transition from PTQ to NTQ transition behavior in between 10 K and 300 K, as a function of anneal temperature. These phenomena can be explained quantitatively by the multi-level model proposed by Shibata et al. [47], which is expressed as,
(2)
I
(
t
)
= I
( 0 )
1 +
∑
n = 1
q
B
n
exp
(
− E
'
n
k
B
T
)
1 +
∑
m = 1
p
A
m
exp
(
−
E
m
k
B
T
)
where I(t) is the PL intensity at any arbitrary temperature T, I(0) is the intensity at 0 K,
E
m
and
E
'
n
are the activation energy of the processes that enhance or reduces the intensity of PL emission, respectively. The numerator describes negative thermal quenching while the denominator refers to the positive thermal quenching. The fitting results are presented in Table 3 . PTQ and NTQ characteristics are dependent on the ratio of the core state to the surface state emission. For as-synthesized (RT) sample, the core state emission is dominant. At a lower measurement temperature, the deep level trap states are frozen and the exciton hopping behavior within the sp2 cluster results in a typical PTQ behavior. The activation energies are found to be 22.49 meV and 3.40 meV for the occurrence of non-radiative processes. The energy required for activating the non-radiative channel is very small. It could be due to the efficient carrier transfer from radiative states to non-radiative states through phonon-assisted tunneling [40].

Thus intensity decreases with increasing measurement temperature. For the annealed samples, a higher density of sp3 hybridized defect states are formed at the surface. Hence the migration of excitons from sp2 to sp3 clusters may result in NTQ behavior in annealed samples. For the sample annealed at 300 οC, excited electrons relax through non-radiative channels and are captured in deep level trap states neat the mid-gap. This leads to low PL emission intensity at a lower temperature. With increase in measurement temperature, the electrons residing in the deep trap states are excited to radiative pathways and the activation energies required for this are 1.28 meV and 203.53 meV. Hence the emission intensity increases with the measurement temperature leading to NTQ behavior. For the samples annealed at intermediate temperatures (100 and 200 οC) there is a crossover from PTQ to NTQ. As shown in Table 3, these two samples have activation energies for both PTQ and NTQ phenomena. The observation of unique negative thermal quenching phenomena opens up a new possibility for higher temperature optical thermometry [48] using GQDs.

As a proof of concept, the photo-detection properties of semiconducting GQDs have been studied by fabricating a lateral Au/GQD/Au (MSM type) photodetector. We have used as-synthesized GQDs (RT) for photodetector, because annealed samples containing higher amount of non-radiative centers act as fast recombination channels. Hence, efficient collection of photo-generated charge carriers will be adversely affected which is undesirable for photodetector applications. A device schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 8 (a). Fig. 8(b) exhibits the current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of the device at both dark and illuminated (by a He-Cd laser source with 325 nm excitation) conditions. The symmetric I-V nature indicates formation of back to back Schottky junctions at the Au/GQD metal-semiconductor interfaces. Current at both dark and illuminated condition shows nearly saturated values at 5 Volt bias. In Fig. 8(c), switching behavior of the device under periodic illumination by a monochromatic He-Cd laser with 325 nm excitation has been shown. This device shows photocurrent to dark current ratio of ∼6 at reverse bias value of −4 V with a fast switching feature.

Hence, GQDs, having good semiconducting properties, are quite promising candidates for UV photodetectors. Further modifications, which include control of carrier concentration and doping levels by functionalization, fabricating p-n junction nanostructured devices is expected to improve performance of the photodetectors, which is being investigated by our group.

We have synthesized size controlled, homogeneous crystalline GQDs from the citric acid by using a one pot rapid and effective chemical route. The synthesized material has been thoroughly characterized by HRTEM, XPS and XRD measurements to establish their structure and crystalline quality. The HRTEM image exhibits clearly visible, well resolved lattice fringes with spacing of 0.24 nm (002 set of planes) and quite clear bright diffraction spots in SAED pattern, indicates the formation of crystalline GQD. XRD pattern exhibits a peak of graphitic carbon and dominance of sp2 carbon bonds over sp3 in XPS confirms the formation of GQDs. The fraction of sp2 over sp3 carbon bonding has been also estimated by XPS analysis and the ratio is found to decrease with increasing thermal annealing temperature. Optical properties of GQDs could be tuned over a broad visible spectral range by modulating the surface functional groups. The emission intensity from the sp2 carbon core is almost independent of the anneal temperature, whereas the emission energy and intensity from the surface groups are found to be strongly temperature dependent. Temperature dependent PL emission exhibits a positive quenching behavior for the as-synthesized samples; whereas, the negative quenching phenomenon is observed for the sample annealed at 300 °C. Intermediate samples exhibit a crossover from PTQ to NTQ behavior with the increase of annealing temperature. Photoresponse characteristics of the semiconducting GQDs exhibiting modulation under switching optical pulses reveal the potential for use in bandgap engineered optical devices. The results indicate that the synthesized luminescent GQDs, with negative thermal quenching characteristics on optimized annealing, are attractive for selective and tunable optical emitters in the visible region, making them useful as potential biomarkers and optical thermometry applications.
There are no conflicts to declare.

T.D. synthesized GQDs and characterized them, fabricated the devices and performed the measurements. S.M. and A.G. helped in synthesis and measurements. T.D., S.M. and S.K.R. planned the work and prepared the manuscript. S.K.R. and S.D. supervised the experiments, analysis and manuscript preparation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Tamal Dey acknowledges DST, Govt. of India for providing INSPIRE Fellowship (IF-160592). Authors acknowledge the partial support from DST-CERI “ICF” project grant. The use of XPS facility under the DST FIST project at Department of Physics, IIT Kharagpur is gratefully acknowledged.
